it was annexed by the United States.<|13.00|> <|13.00|> So it was with great curiosity a few weeks ago that I read a story about an Oklahoma county that formerly belonged to Texas.<|24.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> After a little digging, I discovered that the former Greer County, Texas, no relation, previously belonged to what we know today as Oklahoma as early as 1819, but was claimed by Texas in 1860.<|19.20|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> In 1896, after a protracted dispute between the state of Texas and the federal government,<|8.54|> <|8.54|> the United States Supreme Court decided that Greer County was a territory of the United States,<|17.14|> <|17.14|> as Oklahoma had yet to become a state.<|21.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> In 1906, upon Oklahoma's entry as the 46th state, Greer County became once again a part<|11.20|> <|11.20|> of Oklahoma.<|12.80|> <|12.80|> Comically, at the heart of this land dispute was an 1819 map rife with errors.<|25.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> In her 1995 essay, The Side of Memory, the great American writer Toni Morrison doesn't discuss this phenomenon head-on,<|13.44|> <|13.44|> but she skirts the edges when she says, quote,<|18.66|> <|18.66|> "...the act of imagination is bound up in memory."<|23.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> You know, they straightened out the Mississippi River in places to make room for houses and livable acreage.<|8.78|> <|9.78|> Occasionally, the river notices Cornelius and the other Gentiles speaking in tongues,<|15.94|> <|15.94|> Peter asks a barrier-breaking, border-transgressing, boundary-altering question.<|23.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> flooding is remembering.<|3.24|> <|3.24|> The Holy Spirit will always find its way onto those plains,<|10.12|> <|10.12|> around those bends, and into those crevices it remembers freely flowing in.<|17.96|> <|17.96|> The current of divine mercy can indeed be resisted, but for how long?<|25.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> dams memory and is forever trying to get back to where it was.<|19.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The same can be said of the Holy Spirit.<|2.80|> <|4.12|> God never forgets those places inside of us<|7.08|> <|7.08|> where tenderness, intimacy, and love<|10.84|> <|10.84|> rushed about with abandon.<|12.60|> <|13.56|> And if we are quiet enough,<|15.12|> <|15.80|> we too will remember.<|17.38|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> No border protector is clever enough, no wall is high enough, and no dam is thick enough to keep the water out.<|11.04|> <|11.94|> Just ask Greer County, Texas, or is that Oklahoma?<|17.46|> <|18.30|> Amen.<|19.26|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Amen.<|0.18|><|endoftext|>

segments.text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Depending on who you ask, I am a fifth or sixth generation Texan.<|6.00|>
<|6.00|> My ancestors were brought to the state seven years after it was annexed by the United States.<|13.00|>
<|13.00|> So it was with great curiosity a few weeks ago that I read a story about an Oklahoma county that formerly belonged to Texas.<|24.50|>

<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> After a little digging, I discovered that the former Greer County, Texas, no relation, previously belonged to what we know today as Oklahoma as early as 1819, but was claimed by Texas in 1860.<|19.20|><|endoftext|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> In 1896, after a protracted dispute between the state of Texas and the federal government,<|8.54|>
<|8.54|> the United States Supreme Court decided that Greer County was a territory of the United States,<|17.14|>
<|17.14|> as Oklahoma had yet to become a state.<|21.50|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> In 1906, upon Oklahoma's entry as the 46th state, Greer County became once again a part<|11.20|>
<|12.80|> Comically, at the heart of this land dispute was an 1819 map rife with errors.<|25.20|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> In her 1995 essay, The Side of Memory, the great American writer Toni Morrison doesn't discuss this phenomenon head-on,<|13.44|>
<|13.44|> but she skirts the edges when she says, quote,<|18.66|>
<|18.66|> "...the act of imagination is bound up in memory."<|23.50|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> You know, they straightened out the Mississippi River in places to make room for houses and livable acreage.<|8.78|>
<|9.78|> Occasionally, the river floods these places.<|13.76|>
<|15.26|> Floods is the word they use.<|18.20|>
<|19.08|> But in fact, it is not flooding.<|21.54|>
<|22.30|> It is remembering.<|24.10|>
<|25.38|> Remembering where it used to be.<|27.90|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.56|> All water has a perfect memory and is forever trying to get back to where it was, end quote.<|8.34|>
<|10.60|> Peter is a self-appointed border protector.<|14.38|>
<|15.12|> When he arrives in Caesarea at the home of Cornelius, a Roman centurion and Gentile,<|20.24|>
<|20.24|> he greets him by saying,<|22.34|>
<|22.92|> you know that it is unlawful for a Jew to associate with or visit a Gentile.<|29.50|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But water has a perfect memory and is forever trying to get back to where it was.<|8.24|>
<|9.04|> Peter began speaking to Cornelius and his household about the Jesus who was put to death by hanging on a tree,<|15.72|>
<|16.28|> but whom God raised on the third day, and with whom Peter and a few others ate and drank after he rose from the dead.<|24.50|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> This simple, compelling message resonates with Cornelius and his household.<|6.14|>
<|7.28|> Surprisingly, they began speaking in tongues, just as Jesus' first followers of Jewish descent had done on the day of Pentecost.<|19.26|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> This gift is supposed to remain on one side of the border,<|4.68|>
<|5.42|> not transgress the idea of borders altogether.<|10.18|>
<|11.54|> When he notices Cornelius and the other Gentiles speaking in tongues,<|15.94|>
<|15.94|> Peter asks a barrier-breaking, border-transgressing, boundary-altering question.<|23.30|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And this is according to the message translation.<|2.50|>
<|4.22|> Immediately, Cornelius and his household are baptized<|18.46|>
<|18.46|> because water has a perfect memory<|21.46|>
<|21.46|> and is forever trying to get back to where it was.<|25.96|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> flooding is remembering.<|3.24|>
<|3.24|> The Holy Spirit will always find its way onto those plains,<|10.12|>
<|10.12|> around those bends, and into those crevices it remembers freely flowing in.<|17.96|>
<|17.96|> The current of divine mercy can indeed be resisted, but for how long?<|25.60|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> dams can be built, contracts for wall projects can be signed, but the inevitable will still take place.<|9.64|>
<|9.64|> And what is the inevitable, you might ask?<|12.80|>
<|14.16|> Water has a perfect memory and is forever trying to get back to where it was.<|19.80|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The same can be said of the Holy Spirit.<|2.80|>
<|4.12|> God never forgets those places inside of us<|7.08|>
<|7.08|> where tenderness, intimacy, and love<|10.84|>
<|10.84|> rushed about with abandon.<|12.60|>
<|13.56|> And if we are quiet enough,<|15.12|>
<|15.80|> we too will remember.<|17.38|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> No border protector is clever enough, no wall is high enough, and no dam is thick enough to keep the water out.<|11.04|>
<|11.94|> Just ask Greer County, Texas, or is that Oklahoma?<|17.46|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Amen.<|0.18|><|endoftext|>
